title: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC): An update on risk factors, surveillance, diagnosis and treatment strategies
one_line_summary:
- **HCC** incidence is rising due to **chronic liver disease**.  
- This review covers:  
  - **6‑monthly surveillance** (**ultrasound** + **AFP**)  
  - **Non‑invasive LI‑RADS diagnosis**  
  - **BCLC staging**  
  - **Multidisciplinary management** including:  
    - **Curative** therapies  
    - **Locoregional** therapies  
    - **Systemic immunotherapies**
key_pearls: Ensure all patients with confirmed cirrhosis or high-risk HBV undergo 6-monthly ultrasound and AFP screening; do not surveil those with ECOG PS >= 2 unless transplant candidates.
A LI-RADS 5 (LR-5) designation on contrast-enhanced CT/MRI (lesion > 1 cm with classic arterial hyperenhancement and washout) is diagnostic of HCC in cirrhotic patients; avoid unnecessary biopsies.

Before surgical resection, rigorously assess for Clinically **Significant** Portal Hypertension (CSPH) using indicators like platelet count < 100,000/microL, splenomegaly, varices, or Fibroscan > 25 kPa.
Patients on immunotherapy (Atezolizumab/Bevacizumab or STRIDE) require close monitoring for immune-mediated toxicities and acute-on-chronic liver decompensation.

sections.heading: Epidemiology and Risk Factors
sections.content:
HCC is the 17th most common cancer but the 8th most common cause of cancer-related death in the UK, with a 5-year **survival** rate of only 13% due to late-stage presentation. There is a stark socioeconomic gradient, with death from liver disease up to 5 times more common in the most deprived areas, and HCC incidence strongly associated with the Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD). Key etiologies include:
- Cirrhosis: The strongest independent risk factor for HCC.
- Hepatitis B Virus (HBV): Classified as a Group 1 carcinogen, it can cause HCC in younger, non-cirrhotic patients. Only 50% of HBV-infected individuals in the UK are estimated to be diagnosed.
- Hepatitis C Virus (HCV): Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) achieve cure rates over 95%, but patients with pre-existing advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis remain at long-term risk for HCC and require ongoing surveillance.
- MASLD & MetALD: MASLD has a global prevalence of 30%. The risk is compounded by alcohol consumption (MetALD), making metabolic and alcohol-related liver disease the dominant drivers of HCC in the UK.
- Demographics: Risk increases with older age (steep rise starting at 40 to 44 years) and male sex.

sections.heading: Surveillance and Biomarkers
sections.content: The standard surveillance protocol involves ultrasound and alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) measurement every 6 months. This is **recommended** for patients with cirrhosis and select high-risk non-cirrhotic HBV patients. Surveillance is generally not **recommended** for patients with **significant** comorbidities, severe frailty, an ECOG Performance Status of 2 or above, or decompensated liver disease (unless they are active candidates for liver transplantation). Prognostic models like the aMAP score and PAGE-B (for HBV) help target surveillance to high-risk cohorts. AFP is the only validated biomarker but has a sensitivity of less than 50% when used alone. It is not secreted in up to 40% of cases (especially small tumors) and can be falsely elevated in pregnancy, active hepatitis, or other malignancies.

sections.heading: Non-Invasive Diagnosis and LI-RADS v2018
sections.content:
Unlike most solid tumors, HCC can be diagnosed non-invasively using contrast-enhanced CT or MRI based on characteristic vascular patterns (arterial phase hyperenhancement followed by venous/delayed phase washout). The LI-RADS v2018 framework can only be applied to patients over 18 years old with established cirrhosis or high-risk non-cirrhotic HBV. It cannot be used in patients with vascular liver disorders, cardiac congestion, chronic portal vein thrombosis, or nodular regenerative hyperplasia. LI-RADS categories include:
- LR-NC: Non-categorizable due to image degradation.
- LR-1: Definitely benign.
- LR-2: Probably benign.
- LR-3: Intermediate probability of malignancy.
- LR-4: Probably HCC.
- LR-5: Definitely HCC (lesions must be greater than 1 cm with diagnostic features; allows treatment without biopsy).
- LR-M: Probably or definitely malignant but not HCC-specific.
LI-RADS Treatment Response (LR-TR) categories are: LR-nonviable (no enhancement), LR-equivocal (atypical enhancement), and LR-TR viable (nodular, mass-like, or thick irregular tissue with arterial phase hyperenhancement, washout, or enhancement similar to pre-treatment). Pathology remains **mandatory** for non-cirrhotic patients, equivocal lesions (non-LR-5), and prior to systemic therapy in advanced disease unless otherwise decided by the Multidisciplinary Team (MDT).

sections.heading: Staging and Treatment (BCLC System)
sections.content:
The BCLC Staging system integrates tumor burden (size and number of nodules), liver synthetic function (Child-Pugh class, portal hypertension), and ECOG Performance Status (PS).
- Very Early/Early Stage (BCLC 0/A): Surgical Resection or Ablation offers 5-year **survival** approaching 80% for solitary lesions less than 2 cm, though recurrence rates are 50% to 70% at 5 years. Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy (SABR) is indicated for tumors less than 5 cm or fewer than 6 lesions in patients without extrahepatic disease or hepatic decompensation when standard resection/ablation is unsuitable.
- Intermediate Stage (BCLC B): Locoregional Therapies such as Transarterial chemoembolisation (TACE), bland embolisation (TAE), or selective internal radiation therapy (SIRT) exploit the dual blood supply of the liver (80% portal vein, 20% hepatic artery; HCC is primarily arterially supplied).
- Advanced Stage (BCLC C): First-Line Systemic Therapy with immunotherapy is the standard of care for patients with compensated liver function. Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab (PD-L1 inhibitor + VEGF inhibitor) showed a median overall **survival** (OS) of 19.2 months (IMbrave150 trial). The STRIDE Regimen (single dose of Tremelimumab plus 4-weekly Durvalumab) demonstrates a 5-year overall **survival** rate of 19.6% compared to 9.4% with Sorafenib (HIMALAYA trial).
- Terminal Stage (BCLC D): Best supportive care, with a median **survival** of only 3 to 4 months.

sections.heading: UK Liver Transplantation Criteria
sections.content:
UK Liver Transplantation Listing Criteria include:
- Single lesion less than 5 cm in diameter.
- Up to 5 lesions, all less than or equal to 3 cm.
- Single tumors 5 to 7 cm showing no progression over 6 months.
- AFP level less than 1,000 ng/mL.
Patients modestly outside standard criteria may be listed if they achieve a sustained, favorable response to locoregional therapies (Downstaging). **Absolute** Contraindications include extrahepatic disease, macrovascular invasion, tumor rupture, or AFP greater than 1,000 ng/mL.

strengths_limitations: Strengths: The review provides a highly structured, clinically actionable synthesis of the diagnostic (LI-RADS) and therapeutic (BCLC, UK Transplant) frameworks, emphasizing the dual management of both the oncological process and underlying liver cirrhosis. Limitations: It highlights that clinical trial data supporting the **survival** benefit of surveillance are not robust, and ultrasound sensitivity is **significantly** compromised in obese patients (a major issue in the growing MASLD population).
